class.name: Euler4D
class.brief_description: Represents rotation in 4D using 6 Euler angles.
class.description: Represents rotation in 4D using 6 Euler angles in the order ZX, ZW, XW, YZ, XY, YW. YZ, ZX, and XY are equivalent to rotating around the X, Y, and Z axes in 3D. XW, YW, and ZW are rotations perpendicular to YZ, ZX, and XY, respectively (the ones that share no letters are perpendicular). Perpencular rotations cause something called "double rotation" which is a concept unique to 4D and above. Here's what determines the rotation order of Euler angles. For 3D, we have Yaw, Pitch, and Roll, in that order. Yaw acts as horizontal rotation (ZX plane, no Y so it's horizontal). For the 4D equivalent of Yaw, we have three angles that rotate horizontally (no Y): ZX, ZW, and XW. Therefore, these three go first. More specifically, ZX is yaw-like Yaw, ZW is pitch-like Yaw, and XW is roll-like Yaw, so they goes in the order ZX, ZW, XW. Next is Pitch. Pitch is vertical rotation that changes the direction an object is pointing by moving its forward vector (-Z) up or down (Y), so pitch only exists in the YZ plane. Therefore, for the 4D equivalent of pitch, there is only one angle that does this: YZ. Lastly is Roll. Roll is also vertical rotation, but it brings the sides of an object higher or lower. For the 4D equivalent of roll, we want every angle that involves Y but not Z, so that gives us two angles: XY and YW. Therefore, these two go last.

constructor.name: Euler4D
constructor.description: Constructs a default [Euler4D] representing no rotation, with all components set to 0.

constructor.name: Euler4D
constructor.description: Constructs an Euler4D from 3 rotation angles, in the order YZ, ZX, XY. The XW, YW, and ZW angles are set to 0.

constructor.name: Euler4D
constructor.description: Constructs an Euler4D from 6 rotation angles, in the order YZ, ZX, XY, XW, YW, ZW.

constructor.name: Euler4D
constructor.description: Constructs an Euler4D from 3D euler angles in a [Vector3] in the order YZ, ZX, XY. As such, the only rotation will be in 3D.

constructor.name: Euler4D
constructor.description: Constructs an Euler4D from 3D euler angles in a [Vector3] in the order YZ, ZX, XY, and the second [Vector3] containing the XW, YW, and ZW angles.

method.name: from_basis
method.description: Constructs an [Euler4D] from a [Basis4D] rotation matrix. [b]Note:[/b] Due to the way this is implemented, this process produces a LOT of floating-point error. With 32-bit floats, this has a margin of error of 0.1%.

method.name: is_equal_approx
method.description: Returns [code]true[/code] if this [Euler4D] and [code]euler[/code] are approximately equal, by running [method @GlobalScope.is_equal_approx] on each component.

method.name: to_basis
method.description: Converts this [Euler4D] to a [Basis4D] rotation matrix.

members.member: The rotation on the XW plane. This is perpendicular to YZ rotation, so we treat this as the fourth component. When converting a [Basis4D] to an [Euler4D], this value will be on the range of [code](-PI, PI)[/code].

The rotation on the XY plane. This is equivalent to rotating around the Z axis in 3D, so we treat this as the third component. When converting a [Basis4D] to an [Euler4D], this value will be on the range of [code](-PI/2, PI/2)[/code].

The rotation on the YW plane. This is perpendicular to ZX rotation, so we treat this as the fifth component. When converting a [Basis4D] to an [Euler4D], this value will be on the range of [code](-PI/2, PI/2)[/code].

The rotation on the YZ plane. This is equivalent to rotating around the X axis in 3D, so we treat this as the first component. When converting a [Basis4D] to an [Euler4D], this value will be on the range of [code](-PI/2, PI/2)[/code].

The rotation on the ZW plane. This is perpendicular to XY rotation, so we treat this as the sixth component. When converting a [Basis4D] to an [Euler4D], this value will be on the range of [code](-PI/2, PI/2)[/code].

The rotation on the ZX plane. This is equivalent to rotating around the Y axis in 3D, so we treat this as the second component. When converting a [Basis4D] to an [Euler4D], this value will be on the range of [code](-PI, PI)[/code].

constants.constant: The identity [Euler4D], representing no rotation. All components are set to 0.

operator.name: operator !=
operator.description: Returns [code]true[/code] if the [Euler4D]s are not equal. [b]Note:[/b] Due to floating-point precision errors, consider using [method is_equal_approx] instead, which is more reliable.

operator.name: operator *
operator.description: Returns a new [Euler4D] with each component multiplied by [code]right[/code].

operator.name: operator *
operator.description: Returns a new [Euler4D] with each component multiplied by [code]right[/code].

operator.name: operator +
operator.description: Adds two [Euler4D]s together, which adds each component angle.

operator.name: operator -
operator.description: Subtracts one [Euler4D] from another [Euler4D], which subtracts each component angle.

operator.name: operator ==
operator.description: Returns [code]true[/code] if the [Euler4D]s are exactly equal. [b]Note:[/b] Due to floating-point precision errors, consider using [method is_equal_approx] instead, which is more reliable.

operator.name: operator []
operator.description: Returns the value of the component at the given index. Components are stored in the order YZ, ZX, XY, XW, YW, ZW.

operator.name: operator unary-
operator.description: Returns the negative value of the [Euler4D]. This is the same as writing [code]Euler4D(-e.yz, -e.zx, -e.xy, -e.xw, -e.yw, -e.zw)[/code].
